text: I somewhat disagree (about your statement regarding jump scares). Lemme ask you this: If you plan on scaring a friend or family member, what's the first prank that comes to mind? For most people, I would argue it's jump scaring. And you wouldn't do it every day, right? It'd be once in a while, when they least expect it. You'd do your best to keep them from seeing it coming, hold your breath, camouflage yourself, etc. That's how movie jump scares can be effective. If they're used sparingly, in the right moment, and it's always best to try to keep it as unpredictable as possible. One EXCELLENT example of a good, tactical jump scare that nobody saw coming, was the opening scene of "The Cabin in the Woods." Another would be the "sloth" scene in "Se7en," and another during the blood test scene in "The Thing" (1982), or the dream-telling scene in "Insidious", really I could go on. Jump scares can DEFINITELY be "good" horror, if used correctly. I believe horror shows/movies should use jump scares like they should use CGI: Rarely, thoughtfully, and in the right moments. Essentially, NOT as a crutch ("The Thing" [2011] I'm looking at YOU).

I somewhat disagree (about your statement regarding jump scares). Lemme ask you this: If you plan on scaring a friend or family member, what's the first prank that comes to mind? For most people, I would argue it's jump scaring. And you wouldn't do it every day, right? It'd be once in a while, when they least expect it. You'd do your best to keep them from seeing it coming, hold your breath, camouflage yourself, etc.

That's how movie jump scares can be effective. If they're used sparingly, in the right moment, and it's always best to try to keep it as unpredictable as possible. One EXCELLENT example of a good, tactical jump scare that nobody saw coming, was the opening scene of "The Cabin in the Woods." Another would be the "sloth" scene in "Se7en," and another during the blood test scene in "The Thing" (1982), or the dream-telling scene in "Insidious", really I could go on. 

Jump scares can DEFINITELY be "good" horror, if used correctly. I believe horror shows/movies should use jump scares like they should use CGI: Rarely, thoughtfully, and in the right moments. Essentially, NOT as a crutch ("The Thing" [2011] I'm looking at YOU).
